Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty intense but unconventional. I'd dedicate around 32 hours a week to studying, but most of that happened outside the LMS. I'm an auditory learner, so I'd record lectures or find podcasts and YouTube explanations, then listen to them while commuting or working out. My routine was more about absorbing information through listening than sitting at a computer clicking through modules.
Q2: My pattern varied a lot depending on what was coming up. I'd have these bursts of intense focus when exams approached, but during regular weeks I might skip classes and just catch up through audio materials later. Deadlines definitely drove my schedule more than any fixed weekly routine. Sometimes I'd go days without touching the LMS, then spend a whole weekend catching up on content my own way.
Q3: Usually it was either a deadline notification or needing to check what assignments were due. I didn't log in just to browse or explore content casually. If I got an email reminder about something or realized I needed to submit work, that's when I'd actually open the LMS. Sometimes I'd log in just to download materials so I could study them offline in my preferred format.
Q4: Not really, to be honest. The LMS never became a natural part of my routine because my learning style doesn't mesh well with reading through screens and clicking modules. I tried early in the semester to make it habitual, but I found myself getting more anxious staring at all the incomplete tasks. It was easier to just download what I needed and work with it in ways that suited me better.
Q5: The lecture recordings and any audio materials were gold for me. I could listen to them multiple times, pause, rewind, and really absorb the concepts. Discussion boards were occasionally useful when someone explained something in a different way that clicked. Anything I could convert to audio or listen to passively while doing other things felt most valuable to my learning process.
Q6: I pretty much ignored the reading-heavy PDFs and text-based discussion threads. I'd skim them at best or find alternative audio explanations elsewhere. The interactive quizzes I often delayed because they felt tedious compared to just understanding the material conceptually. If something required a lot of screen time without audio components, I'd find workarounds or just skip it entirely.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if it genuinely helped me understand concepts rather than just checking boxes. If I could listen to something or if it connected to exam content directly, I was in. Busy work that didn't seem to contribute to actual learning felt like a waste, especially when I could spend that time on more effective study methods for my learning style.
Q8: Optional activities felt like they existed for people who learn differently than me. I'd skip most of them unless someone mentioned they were particularly helpful. Required activities I'd complete, but often minimally if they didn't align with how I learn best. There was definitely less guilt about skipping optional stuff, even when my anxiety told me I should be doing everything.
Q9: Absolutely, this was basically my primary mode of studying. I'd download lecture slides, convert them to audio using text-to-speech apps, and listen while walking or doing chores. I also used external YouTube channels, podcasts related to the course topics, and recorded myself summarizing concepts to play back later. My actual learning happened mostly outside the LMS ecosystem.
Q10: Almost all of my exam preparation wouldn't show up in the LMS. I'd spend hours listening to recorded explanations, discussing concepts out loud with myself, or using flashcard apps with audio features. My 88 on exams came from this invisible studying. If you only looked at my LMS activity, you'd think I barely engaged, but I was actually putting in serious hours just in different ways.
Q11: All the time. I'd access a lecture video once, download it or rip the audio, and then replay it dozens of times offline. Same with slides—I'd grab them once and then study from my converted audio versions for weeks. My single access in the LMS didn't reflect the repeated engagement I had with that same material in different formats.
Q12: My anxiety would spike during certain weeks, making it really hard to even open the LMS and face all the incomplete tasks. Seeing that low completion percentage triggered this spiral where I'd avoid it even more. Despite feeling generally happy day-to-day, there were moments when the accumulated stress of seeing what I hadn't done made engagement feel overwhelming rather than productive.
Q13: When I had multiple courses demanding attention simultaneously, the LMS for this course would get neglected first since I'd developed my own offline system. Fatigue definitely played a role—when I was tired, audio learning felt manageable but active LMS engagement felt impossible. My anxiety about incomplete tasks paradoxically made me avoid the platform more, creating this frustrating cycle.
Q14: Yeah, sometimes I'd log in and just scroll through materials without really processing anything. My mind would be elsewhere, worried about other things, and I'd click through videos at 2x speed just to mark them complete. Those sessions were about reducing the visual reminder of incomplete tasks rather than actual learning. Real engagement happened later, offline, when I could focus properly.
Q15: I definitely postponed discussion posts because they required written output that felt unnatural to me. I'd know they were important for participation grades but kept pushing them off because formulating written responses is harder for me than verbal processing. My anxiety about doing them 'right' made the avoidance worse, even though logically I knew just doing something was better than nothing.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me into the LMS consistently. I'd cram submissions right before due dates, which explains my lower assignment completion rate. Without a deadline, I had no urgency to interact with the platform since my learning was happening elsewhere. The deadline pressure would spike my anxiety but also force action I otherwise wouldn't take.
Q17: Feedback honestly didn't change my LMS behavior much because by the time I got it, I'd already moved on to preparing for the next thing. Good feedback on exams validated that my offline methods worked, which actually reinforced my avoidance of heavy LMS use. If feedback suggested I needed to engage more with specific materials, I'd find audio alternatives rather than increasing platform time.
Q18: The course structure with its heavy emphasis on online modules didn't really suit my learning style. I had to work around it rather than with it. The fact that lectures were recorded was a saving grace because I could extract the audio. But the structure assuming everyone learns through reading and clicking through screens made me feel like I was constantly swimming upstream.
Q19: Having downloadable materials made engagement easier because I could adapt them to my needs. What made it harder was when content was only accessible through interactive modules that required being logged in. The discussion requirements felt forced and didn't contribute to my understanding. More audio-native content or podcast-style explanations would have made official engagement much easier for me.
Q20: I think those students might be searching for what they need, trying different approaches, or dealing with anxiety about the course that keeps them checking in without producing. Maybe they're perfectionists who keep reviewing but never feel ready to submit. Or they could be like me in reverse—present on the platform but doing their real productive work somewhere else entirely.
Q21: That's literally my experience. My exam score was 88 and I got a Distinction, but my LMS engagement was minimal. I was learning intensively, just not in ways the system could track. Students who understand their own learning styles and have resources to support alternative methods can absolutely succeed without high platform activity. The LMS isn't the only place learning happens.
Q22: Course logs can accurately show when someone accessed materials and submitted work—the mechanical actions. But they completely miss the quality of engagement, offline studying, alternative resources used, and whether someone actually understood anything. They can't capture me listening to a lecture recording for the fifth time while cooking dinner. The depth of learning is invisible to these systems.
Q23: Being engaged means I'm actively processing and understanding the material, regardless of where or how that happens. It's when concepts click and I can explain them back to myself verbally. True engagement for me involves curiosity and connection to the content, not just logging hours on a platform. Completing tasks isn't engagement if I'm just going through motions without learning.
Q24: The LMS would never show how many hours I spent with audio versions of course content, the external resources I used, or the verbal processing I did to understand concepts. It wouldn't capture my anxiety about the platform itself or how that shaped my avoidance patterns. Most importantly, it couldn't show that despite low visible engagement, I was genuinely learning and ultimately succeeded because I found methods that worked for my brain.
